title: Folklore the Affliction: Fall of the Spire - Part Two: Let the Bookkeeping Begin
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z0A-aCcVXSA

Daniel (@dungeondive) starts Story One, *Into the City*, in Eürotrusk on Dusk difficulty — and uses the setup as the spine of an *ongoing review-by-bookkeeping*. The headline observation: Folklore the Affliction is one of the most bookkeeping-heavy games he has ever owned, second perhaps only to Kingdom Death Monster. Character sheets, session ledgers, encounter logs, ability tokens, status tokens, initiative cards, heirloom cards, companion cards, boon cards — by the time the two heroes are stat-blocked and ready to leave the inn the table has thirty things on it that didn't exist five minutes ago.

He frames his own role accordingly: not the *protagonist*, but the *party manager*, keeping a ledger on the heroes' behalf. The two heroes — Shauna the Druidus (earth-binder rune-inscriber, bearded-dragon companion, silver-pendant thorn aura) and Joanna the Huntress (sacred-attack shaman, mongoose companion, lore-book heirloom granting a free ability point) — get fully kitted out on camera.

He flags a tangentially-related design love: every chit in Folklore has its art on one side and its meaning printed on the other. He'd like to see more games do this. He also surfaces the *Adventure Creation Kit* book from the Farmer's Bundle as a thing he'll return to — a full RPG sandbox manual hidden in a board-game expansion.

----

Daniel describes himself in Folklore as the *party manager* rather than as either hero. Do games of this size benefit from that framing, or does it pull you out of the fiction?
